title: SpaceX: the complete case (quality, mania risk, liquidity, and the decade ahead)
question: Should you buy the SpaceX IPO - at the offer, in the aftermarket, or at all? Across four lenses: is the entry good (quality), is it a retail-mania top (TRUMP/GME analog), is there enough market for it (liquidity + value chain), and can the 'next FAANG' upside even exist (the decade)?
finding: Magnificent company, poor entry - asymmetric payoff. QUALITY: trade-only/wait-for-unlock; 4.25% float, 9.4x supply by day 180, 82% Musk voting control, 94x sales, elite underwriters but harsh float/control/unlock math. MANIA: no on the market mechanism (flow shock ~100x too small to drain the market, unlike the TRUMP coin which marked its host top in 3 days), yes on the share's low-float architecture (same scarcity-then-unlock decay as TRUMP/GME); the mega-IPO chase loses (-27.7% median 1y excess vs S&P, matching study 15's -28.4% broad base). LIQUIDITY: enough in aggregate (smallest relative cash call of the three issuance manias - 2.0% of $7.87T money-market cash) but the lock-up overhang is concentrated on SPCX's own book; value-chain peers bleed by substitution when the leader completes the complex's story (Coinbase peers -29%, Rivian -22%); a bootstrap cone and a blind multi-agent swarm rehearsal independently agree the break is in the back half of the unlock calendar, peaking at the Dec day-180 cleanup, not a market-wide top. DECADE: 'next FAANG' is arithmetically foreclosed - FAANG IPO'd at a ~$1.2B median, SPCX enters at $1,770B; from $2T even the weakest FAANG multiple (Meta 14x) needs $25T = ~5x the largest company ever, so the realistic ceiling is a low-single-digit multiple (3x = today's Nvidia). Consolidates former studies 24+28+29; the swarm engine's general validity is in study 30.
